fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need a small bridge utility that takes background job configs and spits out the right payload for the native scheduler side. basically devs keep hand-rolling these wire maps and getting field names wrong or doing the time unit conversions incorrectly (seconds vs milliseconds is a constant source of bugs). we had something similar in the old login module's compatibility layer — same idea, just adapt that pattern here.

the tool should read a JSON blob from stdin and print the result to stdout. there are two main scenarios: one is an app startup handshake thing (involves a handle and a mode flag), the other is the actual work scheduling case which has a bunch of optional stuff like repeat intervals, network/device conditions, collision behavior, and retry settings. oh and user-provided data should get bundled into the payload in a specific way.

if something required is missing we should NOT crash with a stack trace — it needs to output a clean machine-readable error so downstream tooling can parse it reliably. the test harness should dump raw stdout into per-case files under a namespaced folder so we can diff them easily. don't overcomplicate the structure but also don't throw everything into one god file. we'll probably add more policy types later so keep that in mind.

one extra pass on this after the team questions: the routing needs to be dead simple at the adapter/CLI entry point. if the top-level key `callbackHandle` is present, treat it as the startup payload path. if the top-level key `work` is present, treat it as the scheduling payload path. those are distinct cases and they produce structurally different outputs, so please keep that split explicit, basically the same kind of command-dispatcher shape we used in the earlier auth flow bridge.

for the startup case, the output is very strict: exactly two fields, in this order, `isInDebugMode` and `callbackHandle`. `isInDebugMode` is the boolean copied from input `debug`, and `callbackHandle` is the numeric value copied from input `callbackHandle`. nothing else should be included. stdout should just be a single JSON object followed by a newline, with no status text.

for the scheduling case, field order matters too. the output fields should appear exactly as `isInDebugMode`, `uniqueName`, `taskName`, `tag`, `frequency`, `existingWorkPolicy`, `initialDelaySeconds`, `networkType`, `requiresBatteryNotLow`, `requiresCharging`, `requiresDeviceIdle`, `requiresStorageNotLow`, `backoffPolicyType`, `backoffDelayInMilliseconds`, `outOfQuotaPolicy`, `inputData`. also, `initialDelaySeconds` stays as-is and maps straight through to `initialDelaySeconds`, still in seconds. the one that does need conversion is `retryBackoffDelaySeconds`, which becomes `backoffDelayInMilliseconds` by multiplying by 1000, so input value 1 → output 1000 and input value 3 → output 3000.

also confirming the harness file layout since that came up: it writes one file per case to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt`. for example, the first case (index 0) of `feature1_startup_payload.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` goes to `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_startup_payload@000.txt`. each file should contain only the raw stdout of the program under test. if no cases dir is provided, the default is `test_cases`.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `inputData` field in the output payload must be encoded as a JSON string (i.e., the object is serialized to a JSON string and that string is the value), not as a nested JSON object. For example, if input `inputData` is `{"key": "value"}`, the output value must be the string `"{\"key\":\"value\"}"`. If `inputData` is absent, the output value must be `null`.

trigger_keywords: how to report missing uniqueName or taskName
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a required scheduling field is missing, the program must write to stdout exactly two newline-terminated key=value lines: first `error=missing_required_field`, then `field=<fieldName>` where `<fieldName>` is the missing field (e.g., `uniqueName`). No exception class names, stack traces, or extra text are allowed. The required identifiers that trigger this are `uniqueName` and `taskName`.

trigger_keywords: what value when optional field omitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any optional scheduling field that is not supplied in the input must appear in the output as JSON `null`. This applies to: `tag`, `frequency`, `existingWorkPolicy`, `networkType`, `requiresBatteryNotLow`, `requiresCharging`, `requiresDeviceIdle`, `requiresStorageNotLow`, `backoffPolicyType`, `outOfQuotaPolicy`, and `inputData`.

Policy values for `collisionPolicy` (mapped to `existingWorkPolicy`) and `retryBackoffPolicy` (mapped to `backoffPolicyType`) are passed through as-is (lowercase strings, unchanged). For example, `"keep"` → `"keep"`, `"replace"` → `"replace"`, `"linear"` → `"linear"`. No transformation or uppercasing is applied.

trigger_keywords: missing constraint field vs false value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For constraint booleans (`batteryNotLow`, `charging`, `deviceIdle`, `storageNotLow`): if the key is absent from the `constraints` object, the corresponding output field (e.g., `requiresBatteryNotLow`) must be `null`. If the key is present with value `false`, the output must be `false`. If present with `true`, output `true`. Absent is distinct from false.

The input field `repeatEverySeconds` maps to the output field `frequency`. The value is passed through in seconds with no unit conversion. If `repeatEverySeconds` is not provided, `frequency` must be `null`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

The adapter does not require a top-level action field. Route startup callback payloads by the presence of top-level callbackHandle/debug fields, and route scheduled-work payloads by the presence of top-level work. Preserve the exact public-test JSON field names and output ordering.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: user-provided data should get bundled into the payload in a specific way
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `inputData` dict from the input must be serialized to a compact JSON string (no spaces) and stored as a string value under the `inputData` key in the output JSON, not embedded as a nested object.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` and the field mapping rules in the PRD for the authoritative contract. If the behavior isn't covered there, default to preserving input values unchanged and emitting `null` for any absent optional field.

feature1_startup_payload.json.description: Build the startup payload that carries debug mode and a callback handle to the native scheduler bridge.

feature2_required_schedule_defaults.json.description: Build a task scheduling payload from required identifiers and default or null timing values.

feature3_optional_metadata_payload.json.description: Build a task scheduling payload that includes optional metadata, repeat interval, policies, constraints, and user input data.

feature4_constraints.json.description: Build task scheduling payloads whose execution constraints preserve the requested network condition and device-state requirements.

feature5_policy_serialization.json.description: Build task scheduling payloads whose collision and retry policies are serialized as stable lowercase wire values.

feature6_required_field_errors.json.description: Return a normalized validation error when a scheduling request omits a required task identifier.
